title: A Lavish New Adaptation of The Master and Margarita
content:
Mikhail Bulgakov’s legendary novel *The Master and Margarita* is such a blistering critique of authoritarianism that it seems baffling how a new film adaptation was ever approved in modern Russia. Written in the 1930s, the Soviet-era cult classic exposes the hypocrisy of a totalitarian regime by mocking its culture of censorship and persecution. The narrative weaves together fantastical characters, absurdist humor, and a stark portrayal of an oppressive society. Director Michael Lockshin initially hesitated to adapt the script, but he reconsidered due to its undeniable relevance to the current political climate. He successfully translates the book's most flamboyant and hilarious moments to the screen, including a wild black magic show on a Moscow stage.

In the film, the authorities eventually catch up with the titular Master, who desperately throws his manuscript into a fire as they storm his apartment. This dramatic confrontation prompts the devilish character Woland to deliver the legendary one-liner, “Manuscripts don’t burn.” When the Master’s beloved Margarita cannot find him, she forms a pact with the devil and hosts a demonic grand ball for notorious evildoers. The director finishes the story with a bombastic dual ending where the manuscript survives, but the city of Moscow burns. Over five million Russian citizens bought tickets to see this defiant slap against regimes that curtail human rights.

Despite its massive domestic success, the film's international future remains highly uncertain due to geopolitical and legal complexities. Lockshin, who relocated to the United States before the full-scale invasion of Ukraine, has faced immense backlash from Russian authorities for his anti-regime comments. Additionally, a complicated legal dispute over the novel's copyright threatens to block the film's release in American cinemas. Meanwhile, Ukrainian audiences reject Bulgakov entirely, viewing him as a Russian nationalist who historically looked down upon their culture. Consequently, this remarkable cinematic achievement may be effectively banned from theaters across the globe.
